_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Cardiovascular: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
condition: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
points: Identify ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) quickly
Determine the time of symptom onset
Activate hospital-based STEMI system of care
Monitor vital signs and cardiac rhythm and be prepared to provide CPR and defibrillation if needed
Administer appropriate medications
Transport to appropriate facility

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Chest pain or discomfort in other areas of the body (e.g., arm, jaw, epigastrium) of suspected cardiac origin, shortness of breath, associated or unexplained sweating, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness. Atypical or unusual symptoms are more common in women, the elderly, and diabetic patients. May also present with CHF, syncope, and/or shock
Chest pain associated sympathomimetic use (e.g., cocaine, methamphetamine)

Some patients will present with likely non-cardiac chest pain and otherwise have a low likelihood of ACS (e.g., blunt trauma to the chest of a child). For these patients, defer the administration of aspirin (ASA) and nitrates per the Pain Management Guideline
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety; position patient in position of comfort (upright or semi-recumbent)
Oxygen via NRM if SpO2 <94% or severe distress
Assist patient with own prescribed nitroglycerin if available
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if suspected cause
CPR and AED if patient becomes pulseless

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Aspirin 162-325 mg PO chewable non-enteric for suspected ischemic chest pain
Patient's own prescribed nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q 3-5 min if SBP >100 mmHg
12-lead ECG acquisition within 10 minutes of patient contact; transmit to receiving facility
CPAP for acute pulmonary edema/CHF presentation
Pulse oximetry continuous monitoring
IV maintenance (non-medicated) if existing access

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL (agency stock) q 3-5 min if SBP >100; avoid with phosphodiesterase inhibitors in past 48 hours
IV access initiation; IV fluid bolus 250-500 mL NS for hypotension (avoid in pulmonary edema)
Waveform capnography
IO access if IV unsuccessful and patient critical

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation: identify STEMI, LBBB, posterior MI, right ventricular involvement
Advance notification to PCI center for STEMI (target: first medical contact to balloon <90 min)
Morphine sulfate 2-4 mg IV for pain unresponsive to nitrates (use cautiously in NSTEMI)
Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV as alternative analgesic
Antidysrhythmic therapy for associated arrhythmia per protocol
RSI and intubation for airway compromise or cardiogenic shock
Vasopressors (dopamine, norepinephrine) for cardiogenic shock not responsive to fluids

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension
Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted)
Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients.
Consider configuring monitor/defibrillator to allow automatic VT/VF alert
Consider configuring monitor/defibrillator to allow ST-segment trending if available

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Key Considerations
Acute coronary syndrome may present with atypical pain, vague or only generalized complaints.
Ischemic burden time is a risk for morbidity and mortality, EMS can help decrease first medical contact to intervention time/reflow by efficient scripting/training of safely minimizing scene time
Pertinent Assessment Findings

A complete medication list should be obtained from each patient. It is especially important for the treating physician and healthcare providers to be informed if the patient is taking beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, clonidine, digoxin, blood thinners (anticoagulants), and medications for the treatment of erectile dysfunction or pulmonary hypertension

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' The time of symptom onset
' The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition
' The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given
' The time of STEMI notification

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension

Chest pain or discomfort in other areas of the body (e.g., arm, jaw, epigastrium) of suspected cardiac origin, shortness of breath, associated or unexplained sweating, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness. Atypical or unusual symptoms are more common in women, the elderly, and diabetic patients. May also present with CHF, syncope, and/or shock
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
